nodes.text: On-call Rotation

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The original storage engine author still reviews every schema migration before it merges.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The team prefers small, reversible pull requests over large rewrites because rollbacks are cheaper to reason about.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: There is one primary point of contact for questions about the on-disk format and the write-ahead log.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: An engineer flagged that the nightly backup job was silently truncating large snapshots and pushed the fix within a day.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The billing service has a single owner end to end, from invoicing logic to the Stripe webhook handlers.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The billing owner insists every change ships behind a feature flag because refunds are expensive to undo.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The billing owner rewrote the nightly reconciliation job so retried webhooks stop double-charging customers.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The billing owner is on parental leave through September; a colleague is covering escalations until she returns.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The search service has an owner who tuned the BM25 ranking weights last quarter.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The search index was rebuilding on every single deploy, tripling deploy time, until the rebuild was discovered and moved off the deploy path into an async job.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The search owner pairs best in the mornings; schedule design reviews before noon for sharp feedback.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The on-call lead rewrote the escalation policy after last winter's paging storm.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The on-call lead is the fastest responder to production incidents on the team and keeps a running postmortem log.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The on-call lead covers billing escalations while the usual owner is on leave, so route Stripe disputes accordingly for now.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The ingestion pipeline has an owner who rewrote the Kafka consumer to handle partition rebalances gracefully.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The pipeline owner decided to cap ingestion batch size at five thousand records after a burst of oversized payloads stalled consumers.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The pipeline owner is skeptical of adding another queue; the existing Kafka topics already cover it.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We chose redb over SQLite for the storage engine because it gives us a single-writer MVCC B-tree without a C dependency.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Zero-copy reads let the engine memory-map pages directly instead of deserializing every record on access.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The redb upgrade to the v2 file format required a one-time migration pass over every table on first open.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: There is no support for concurrent writers, so the engine funnels every write through a single applier thread.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: SQLite was the original prototype backend before the switch to redb; it is kept only in the legacy migration tests.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We never shipped SQLite to production because its file locking caused timeouts under concurrent agent writes.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: An early database candidate's WAL mode was tempting, but the lack of a typed schema made property indexing awkward and slowed queries.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The analytics warehouse mirrors write events into Postgres nightly so the data team can run ad-hoc SQL.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Postgres is not on the hot write path; it only receives a batched, eventually-consistent copy of the graph.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We decided against Postgres as the primary store because we needed embedded, single-file deployment for agents running on laptops.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Kafka backs the ingestion pipeline's event bus; each topic is partitioned by scope id for locality.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We lost three hours of events last spring when a Kafka broker ran out of disk; retention is now capped tighter.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Kafka consumer lag over five minutes triggers a page to whoever is on call that week.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The billing service's invoice generator runs at 2am UTC daily and emails a summary if it fails twice in a row.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Billing has a hard constraint: no refund logic may run without a second engineer's sign-off in the pull request.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The billing service migrated off its legacy tax table last spring after two states changed their rates mid-quarter.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Never deploy changes on the last day of the month; that's when transaction volume peaks and rollbacks are riskiest.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The login password rotates every ninety days and users get a heads-up email one week ahead.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We chose to store session tokens with a short fifteen-minute expiry and silent refresh instead of long-lived cookies.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Auth service outages count as sev-1 incidents because every other service depends on it to validate requests.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Never run the auth service migration during peak hours; the last attempt locked the sessions table for six minutes.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The search service's ranking pipeline blends BM25 text relevance with a vector leg and a lightweight graph boost.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Search latency regressed after the last index rebuild; it was traced to a missing composite index on scope id.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The most heavily load-tested ranking pipeline component needed tuning after a latency regression, since every recall call touches it on the hot path.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The ingestion pipeline validates every incoming record against its schema before it reaches the write queue.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: A schema decision added an unindexed lookup on every record, dropping throughput by half.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Failed writes retry three times with exponential backoff before a record is parked in the dead-letter topic.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The CI pipeline runs the full differential fuzz suite on every merge to main, not just on release branches.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We decided against nightly-only fuzz runs after a corruption bug sat undetected in main for four days.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: It takes eleven minutes end to end, fast enough to run on every merge instead of batching into a nightly run; anything slower gets flagged as a build health regression.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Staging mirrors production's index configuration exactly so a schema regression shows up before it ever reaches customers.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Nobody may skip the staging deploy step, even for a one-line hotfix; two outages started as bypassed staging checks.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Production runs on three availability zones with automatic failover if the primary zone's health check fails twice.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We had a four-hour outage last winter when a bad migration locked the primary table during peak traffic.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Production capacity planning assumes ten times the current write volume to leave headroom for a big customer's launch.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The vector index stores bge-small embeddings per node and is rebuilt in the background whenever the model changes.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Cosine similarity lookups over the vector index are capped at a candidate depth so a huge k can't scan the whole corpus.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We picked a 384-dimension embedding model as the default because it keeps the vector index small enough to fit in memory for most workloads.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The on-call rotation is now two weeks long instead of one after the team found one week caused too much handoff churn.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Anyone paged for a sev-1 must post an initial status update within ten minutes, even if it's just 'still investigating'.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The on-call runbook was rewritten after a postmortem showed the old steps referenced a decommissioned dashboard.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: We standardized on conventional commits across every repo so the changelog can be generated automatically.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The team retro cadence moved from weekly to biweekly after feedback that weekly retros surfaced the same three issues repeatedly.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: Document review turnaround is capped at two business days to keep design proposals from stalling in review.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.text: The office snack budget was cut in half this quarter to fund an extra load-testing environment instead.

queries.query: storage engine reviewer
reconciliation job double charging
search index rebuilding tripling deploy time
kafka consumer partition rebalance
invoice generator failure email
auth migration peak hours locked sessions
fuzz suite merge to main
staging deploy skip hotfix
vector index rebuild background model change
on-call rotation two weeks handoff
credentials expiry schedule
when the login system goes down every other system breaks too
a message queue crashed after running low on storage
a flawed rollout locked the main datastore at the worst possible time
who is responsible for adjusting relevance scoring parameters
an early database candidate accepted any shape of entry, making queries clumsy
vcs convention behind auto-generated release notes
db choice without a C library
perf dip linked to a scope lookup gap
ci discipline after an undetected defect lingered for days
sign-off requirements before releasing billing changes
on-call shift length and what triggers an escalation policy change
ranking pipeline tuning and latency regressions in the search stack
batch sizing and Kafka consumer rebalancing decisions for the pipeline
why we run the full fuzz suite on every merge instead of only nightly
